html:
1538.TW: A Distressed Stub With a Balance-Sheet Catalyst

Jenn Feng is not the renewable infrastructure owner described in the prior drafts. The “Jenn Feng New Energy” label is mostly a legacy naming artifact: the company was formerly Jenn Feng New Energy and changed back to Jenn Feng Industrial Tools in 2020, while today it still sells tools, lighting, green-energy equipment, inverters, aquaculture equipment, and energy-storage planning services. That makes the real thesis more interesting, and also riskier: this is a tiny, distressed Taiwan-listed industrial reboot with a signed asset-sale catalyst larger than the market cap, not a clean contracted solar IPP.

At the May 15, 2026 close of TWD 12.40, the equity value is about TWD 400m, roughly US$12m, not US$400m. The mismatch matters. The stock trades under full-cash delivery status, with only 11 lots traded that day, no P/E, and a 2025 EPS loss of TWD 1.59. Screens reject it automatically. Retail memory is also toxic: old solar hype threads, a former-chairman stock-manipulation case, and comments about full-delivery status dominate the footprint. This is exactly why the setup exists.

The financials are ugly on the surface. 2025 revenue was only TWD 114.8m, gross profit was TWD 26.5m, operating loss was TWD 51.1m, operating cash flow was negative TWD 64.4m, and year-end cash was TWD 20.3m. Q1 2026 got worse: revenue fell to TWD 10.4m, net loss was TWD 35.7m, EPS was negative TWD 1.11, and equity attributable to parent holders fell to TWD 125.8m. April revenue rebounded to TWD 9.21m, up 130.6% month over month and 7.7% year over year, but four-month revenue was still down 49.0%.

The variant perception is that the market is pricing the company as if it is simply bleeding toward another dilutive rescue. That may be true in the bear case, but it ignores the signed disposal of JFG International. Jenn Feng has signed an agreement to sell 100% of JFG International, including Shanghai Jenn Feng and Jenn Feng International BVI but excluding Samoa Hongshi and Kunshan Zhengfu, for US$13.84m, roughly TWD 450m at recent FX. The expected accounting gain is small, only about TWD 4.3m, so screeners do not see a profit catalyst. The actual importance is liquidity: a gross proceeds figure that is larger than the entire current market cap can change survival odds, clean up the structure, and fund the new operating plan.

The second asset action is the sale of 96.3% of Cheng Feng Energy Industrial for not less than TWD 26m to a related party. That cuts against the old “hidden renewable infrastructure portfolio” thesis, but supports the cleaner thesis: management is monetizing or pruning non-core holdings and trying to refocus the listed company around a smaller operating platform. The official product story is now green-energy equipment integration, inverters, aquaculture facilities, lighting, and low-carbon power tools, not ownership of power plants.

The forward case is not based on trailing earnings. It is based on a recapitalized microcap that can move from distress valuation to option value. Jenn Feng already proved in 2021 to 2023 that annual revenue can be above TWD 190m to TWD 286m. If the remaining company can recover toward TWD 250m to TWD 300m of revenue with mid-20s gross margin and a reduced cost base, operating break-even is plausible. If green-energy equipment and aquaculture-energy products gain traction, the revenue ceiling is higher because the addressable market is broader than legacy tools. The operating leverage is sharp because the market cap is only TWD 400m and the share count is 32.3m.

The private financing authorization is the swing factor. The board is again seeking the ability to issue private preferred shares or convertible bonds within a 30m-share-equivalent limit, with the major shareholder Baishifu Investment named as a possible subscriber. That is nearly the size of the existing share base, so it can either destroy minorities or save the balance sheet. In the base case, insiders backstop working capital after the JFG sale closes, the company exits the cash-crisis narrative, and the equity re-rates from “full-delivery loss maker” to “tiny restructuring stub with funded optionality.” In the bear case, the same mechanism becomes a discounted control transfer.

The legal overhang is not gone, but it is more bounded. The former chairman was convicted in connection with the 2022 private placement case, with reported company damages of TWD 11.7m, and Jenn Feng separately announced a settlement with him. Governance remains a real discount, but the key point is that the scandal belongs to the prior control era. Current chairman Chen Yung-Lun’s job is now brutally simple: close the JFG sale, collect cash, stop the operating bleed, and prove the green-equipment pivot with monthly revenue.

At TWD 12.40, this is not optically cheap on book. It is more than 3x post-Q1 book value. The cheapness is instead event-adjusted. The stock is priced as if the JFG sale has low closing probability or little liquidity value. If that is wrong, the equity has obvious torque because the gross proceeds are roughly the current market cap, the float is tiny, and the stock is institutionally unownable until the balance sheet and trading status improve.

The cleanest way to own the thesis is to treat it as a recapitalization call option, not a compounder. I would not underwrite value from fantasy solar PPAs or battery-storage assets. I would underwrite value from three things: the JFG cash event, the reduction of legacy complexity, and the possibility that a very small revenue base turns with any credible green-equipment order flow.

Bear case: TWD 5, down 60% from TWD 12.40. The JFG sale slips or fails, private financing is highly dilutive, Q2 and Q3 revenue stay weak, and the stock remains full-delivery with book value eroding.

Base case: TWD 22, up 77%. The JFG sale closes, liquidity risk fades, insiders or a strategic investor fund the reboot on tolerable terms, and revenue recovers toward a TWD 200m to TWD 250m annual run rate with losses narrowing.

Bull case: TWD 38, up 206%. The sale proceeds are collected, full-delivery status is eventually removed, monthly revenue confirms the green-equipment pivot, and the market capitalizes Jenn Feng as a funded Taiwan microcap turnaround rather than a dying tool company.
